data.title: Northrop Grumman Systems Gets $885M U.S. Army Tank-Round Pact
data.description:
By Adam L. Cataldo

Northrop Grumman Systems received a $885 million contract to supply tank rounds to the U.S. Army.

The company will provide 120mm Advanced Multi-Purpose M1147 High Explosive Multi-Purpose with Tracer Cartridge for the army and for foreign military sales in the future, the Defense Department said in a statement on Friday.

Bids were solicited through the internet with one offer received, the Pentagon said.

The contract is for a cost-plus-fixed-fee, firm-fixed-price, and fixed-price with economic price adjustment, according to the Pentagon.

Work locations and funding will be determined with each order, with an estimated completion date of March 28, 2031.

The Army Contracting Command in Newark, N.J., is the contracting activity.

Write to Adam L. Cataldo at adam.cataldo@wsj.com

data.title: Court Hands Argentina's Milei a Victory, Rejecting $16 Billion Verdict — WSJ
data.description:
By Ryan Dubé

A U.S. federal appeals court ruled Friday that Argentina doesn't have to pay shareholders $16 billion for its takeover of the country's biggest oil company, handing President Javier Milei a victory that will help his free-market overhaul.

Milei's cash-strapped government would have struggled to pay the judgment as it undertakes a painful austerity program to overhaul Argentina's economy. The ruling allows the Milei government to continue building its reserves of U.S. dollars, which is crucial to stabilize the economy and attract investments.

"We won the YPF lawsuit," said Milei, a close ally of the Trump administration, on X. He called the ruling "historic, unthinkable, the greatest legal achievement in national history."

Shares of Burford Capital, the litigation financier which had taken on a lawsuit on behalf of the investors, were down 36% on Friday in New York. Burford didn't immediately respond to requests for comment.

The decision by the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit reverses a 2023 ruling by U.S. District Judge Loretta Preska, who determined that two YPF shareholders were owed compensation for the 2012 expropriation. The court of appeals said that Preska incorrectly interpreted Argentine law and that the shareholders' claims are without merit. The court said the $16 billion award equaled about 45% of Argentina's fiscal budget in 2024.

The legal victory was rare for Argentina, which has for years faced and lost lawsuits after breaching contracts, from expropriating companies to defaulting on bonds. The ruling comes as Milei's government has seen its approval slide since a major victory in October's congressional election amid growing scandals involving top officials and a sluggish economy.

While the shareholders could still challenge the ruling at the Supreme Court, Argentina can now "see the light at the end of the tunnel" on a lawsuit that would have undercut its public finances, said Sebastian Maril, an Argentine legal expert who has been closely tracking the YPF case.

"In a country with fragile international reserves, lack of access to capital markets, raising that kind of money would be very difficult for Argentina," said Maril. "This was the big decision that everybody was waiting for."

YPF was expropriated in 2012 under then-president Cristina Kirchner, a left-wing firebrand who is currently under house arrest after corruption charges. The government ended up paying $5 billion in bonds as compensation to YPF's former top shareholder, Spain's Repsol. But smaller shareholders, including now closed U.S. hedge fund Eton Park Capital Management, said they were left out by the Argentine government and sued in federal court in New York. Burford provided the financing and would have taken part of the award.

The Kirchner administration had said that underinvestment by private oil companies justified a state takeover, which Argentina's Congress approved. Opponents of the government said the YPF expropriation was a brazen attempt to take control of promising new oil and gas developments in the country's Vaca Muerta basin.

While celebrating the ruling, Milei lashed out at his leftist opponents who oversaw the expropriation, particularly Axel Kicillof, the former economy minister under Kirchner who is now a top Milei rival as governor of Buenos Aires province.

"We had to come fix the messes," said Milei. He added that the court decision will remove uncertainties surrounding YPF, paving the way for more investments in Argentina's already booming energy sector.

"Today, Argentina avoided one of the largest potential expenditures in its history thanks to professional, strategic action aligned with the public interest," he said.

Write to Ryan Dubé at ryan.dube@wsj.com

data.title: Charles Schwab CEO Richard Wurster Made $18.8M in 2025
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Charles Schwab Chief Executive Richard Wurster made a total of $18.8 million in 2025.

Wurster, who became chief executive in January 2025, saw his pay increase from $12.5 million the year before.

The value of Wurster's stock awards rose by $2.8 million, making up $7.6 million of his total compensation. His base salary, option awards and non-equity incentive plan compensation also rose.

Michael Verdeschi, the bank's chief financial officer, made $8.1 million, up from $6.6 million the year before.

Co-Chair Charles Schwab made roughly the same as he did in 2024. Walt Bettinger, who retired as chief executive at the end of 2024 and is now a co-chair, saw his pay decline by about $20 million to $6.3 million.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Toronto police responding to reports of fire, possible explosions on roof of building
data.description:
Toronto police on Friday said they were responding to reports of a fire on top of a building undergoing construction with possible explosions, as a billowing plume of smoke could be seen rising from a downtown building.

The building is located in a densely populated area close to the city's financial district.

Toronto Paramedic Service said emergency personnel had arrived on scene and had initially encountered no one injured.

data.title: Jamie Dimon Still Has More to Give — Barrons.com
data.description:
To the Editor: JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon has reached an age when retirement might traditionally make sense, and yet clearly he still has the capacity to make a meaningful impact ( "Jamie Dimon Sees an End on the Horizon. But Not Yet," Cover Story, March 20). Retirement, as traditionally defined, needs rethinking. People are living longer, more active lives.

I have worked at firms with financial advisors well into their 80s and even 90s who remained sharp, energetic, and deeply connected to longstanding clients. Rather than fully stepping away, they brought in younger talent and gradually shifted away from tasks better suited to others. This appears to be the path that Dimon is considering and one that many baby boomers and Gen Xers should, too.

Jonathan I. Shenkman West Hempstead, N.Y.

To the Editor: In today's environment of private-market growth, massive capital flows, and explosion of family offices, JPMorgan's success should be viewed as proof that scale, discipline, and leadership still matter. Dimon clearly has run a tight ship, but even a tight ship can become overly mechanical if it stops renewing talent and thinking beyond its own process. That's where many institutions begin to lose culture, even while the numbers still look strong.

Vincenzo Conte On Barrons.com

Sizing Up Tesla

To the Editor: In Streetwise, Jack Hough quotes UBS analyst Joseph Spak asking whether car-delivery metrics even matter to Tesla's valuation ( "What $4 Gasoline Means for GM, Ford, and Tesla," March 20). They should. Even though robo-taxis and humanoid robots are touted as the sexy new things, I'd be cautious about denigrating Tesla's success in cars by moving the goal posts and insisting that the valuation should be multiples of what is justified by the car business alone. Chinese competitors have chased Tesla out of China and may do so in Europe, too. Lots of manufacturers make electric cars and compete with Tesla in the U.S. What if the markets for robo-taxis and humanoid robots turn out the same way?

Jim Hemenway Niwot, Colo.

Safer Than 60/40

To the Editor: Elizabeth O'Brien recommended a 60/40 stock/bond allocation as a sturdy approach to market volatility, stating that in the early 1970s, such a portfolio only "declined 39% to the stock market's 52%" ( "The Fed Expects Inflation to Stay High. Here Are Moves to Make With Your Portfolio," Retirement, March 18). I don't think that a retiree or near-retiree would be at all happy with a 39% decline.

A safer approach would be to raise your cash percentage. You can also hedge your long stock positions that have large capital gains by adding exchange-traded funds that short the indexes. These will have much less volatility than shorting individual stocks.

Harvey Rosen Brooklyn, N.Y

Send letters to: mail@barrons.com. To be considered for publication, correspondence must bear the writer's name, address, and phone number. Letters are subject to editing.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Crypto Prices Under Pressure As Bond Market Stress Overtakes Oil Shock
data.description:
Crypto prices came under pressure again on Friday as Treasury yields, not crude, became the macro variable traders could not ignore. Bitcoin slipped back below $69,000 after a short-lived relief rally earlier this week, while ether also traded lower, as hopes for a near-term easing in the Iran conflict faded and the US 10-year yield stayed near 4.42%.

That is the core argument The Kobeissi Letter pushed in a widely shared thread via X: the market’s center of gravity has shifted from the oil spike itself to the rates shock that follows it. “The bond market is, by far, the biggest problem for the US right now, much bigger than the energy price situation,” Adam Kobeissi wrote.

In the longer note, the firm sharpened the point further: “For weeks, markets have been fixated on oil, war headlines, and geopolitical escalation. But beneath the surface, a much larger force has been building, and it’s now beginning to take control. The bond market is now dictating the path of equities, commodities, and ultimately, policy itself.”

The market action this week fits that thesis. On Thursday, President Donald Trump said he would pause attacks on Iran’s energy plants for 10 days, until April 6, saying talks were “going very well.” Yields initially eased on the headline, but the move did not hold.

By the end of the session, the 10-year Treasury yield had climbed to 4.415%, the highest since July, while mortgage rates had already risen to their highest since October and Fed Governor Lisa Cook said the war had shifted the balance of risks toward inflation. Futures markets had moved to price virtually no chance of a Fed cut in 2026.

And the data shows the stress. The MOVE Index, a gauge of Treasury volatility, is at 115.02, up 17.86% on the day. Kobeissi also showed a FedWatch distribution that, in Kobeissi’s reading, now points to a base case of rates staying broadly unchanged through September 2027, a dramatic reversal from late 2025, when markets were still debating how many cuts the Fed would deliver in 2026.

The Kobeissi Letter
@KobeissiLetter

This is truly historic:

In just 27 days of the Iran War, the discussion has now become about Fed rate HIKES.

Just weeks ago, investors were debating how many rate cuts the Fed would implement in 2026.

Now? There’s a 48% chance of an interest rate HIKE by January 2027.

And,… https://t.co/ccQ91LLH3g pic.twitter.com/ve2drzl4Rb

Mar 26, 2026

The firm tied that repricing to a labor market it says has deteriorated even before the latest inflation shock, citing deep downward revisions to payroll data over the last three years and a February unemployment duration of 25.7 weeks.

For crypto, the message is straightforward: this is still trading as a liquidity-sensitive macro asset class. When Trump first said on March 23 that the US would postpone strikes and pursue talks, bitcoin rallied more than 5% to as high as $71,794 in New York, with altcoins also moving higher. That relief move has since unwound. By Friday, bitcoin was trading at $68,639 and ether at $2,061.81, both down on the day as the market rotated back to yields, policy risk and tighter financial conditions.

BitMEX co-founder Arthur Hayes framed the crypto angle more directly in his usual shorthand. “Almost there … If Trump invades Iran what is Buffalo Bill Bessent going to do to calm the UST market?” he wrote, referring to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent.

Arthur Hayes
@CryptoHayes

Almost there …

If Trump invades Iran what is Buffalo Bill Bessent going to do to calm the UST market? pic.twitter.com/7H2qakadgT

Mar 26, 2026

The point is not simply that war could rattle markets, but that a deeper selloff in Treasuries could force some form of response from Washington. In Hayes’ macro framework, crypto does not meaningfully recover just because geopolitical tensions ease; it recovers when bond-market stress becomes severe enough to bring liquidity back into the system, whether through Bessent, the Fed, or both.

Kobeissi’s framework is similar. The firm argues that as yields move toward the 4.50% to 4.70% range on the 10-year, the odds of some form of policy response rise sharply because the White House has already shown it is sensitive to bond-market stress.

That leaves crypto watching the same dashboard as every macro desk: Treasury yields, rate expectations and the credibility of any de-escalation headline. If bond volatility cools, crypto assets could respond the way they did earlier this week, snapping higher on even a modest improvement in war headlines.

But if yields continue grinding upward, the market may keep treating bitcoin and the rest of crypto less as geopolitical hedges than as another expression of the global rates trade.

At press time, the total crypto market cap stood at $

Total crypto market cap chart, 1-week chart | Source: TOTAL on TradingView.com

ds

data.title: BlackRock CEO Larry Fink Made $37.7 Million in 2025
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

BlackRock Chief Executive Officer Larry Fink made $37.7 million in 2025, a roughly $700,000 increase from the year before.

Fink's pay was boosted by higher stock awards and about $500,000 in other miscellaneous compensation, according to a Securities and Exchange Commission filing on Friday.

The value of Fink's stock awards increased to $24.6 million from $18.2 million the year before, a 35% increase.

President Robert Kapito's compensation also increased hitting $25.7 million, up from $21.7 million in 2024.

Chief Operating Officer Robert Goldstein made $15.6 million, up from $13.1 million in the prior year.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Arm Boost Earnings Power, Analyst Says. Plus, Chewy, Quanta, and More Stocks. — Barrons.com
data.description:
These reports, excerpted and edited by Barron's, were issued recently by investment and research firms. The reports are a sampling of analysts' thinking; they should not be considered the views or recommendations of Barron's. Some of the reports' issuers have provided, or hope to provide, investment-banking or other services to the companies being analyzed.

Chewy — CHWY-NYSE Outperform — $23.45 on March 25 by Mizuho All of the drama around a conservative guide from Chewy didn't materialize. Shares are seeing a breakout move, up over 10%.

Management sounded increasingly confident on our callback: 1) Incremental Ebitda margins of about 18% seem durable beyond fiscal-year 2026, with potential for L-T targets to be achieved faster than plan, helped by $50 million-plus from AI-related cost savings; 2) Management opened the door to "inorganic" growth within the vet business, ahead of a more detailed segment update slated for April; 3). The SmartEquine revenue contribution is only $80 million this year (buy-side expectations closer to about $130 million), implying organic revenue growth of 8.5% at the high end of guidance; 4) The lighter first-quarter revenue range strictly reflects comparison issues...and 5) The first quarter also embeds a "lower-single digit-millions" impact from higher freight costs.

We'd buy into strength. Target price: $50.

Quanta Services — PWR-NYSE Outperform — $578.44 on March 25 by Evercore ISI We are bullish on Quanta Services shares, as power and the associated infrastructure buildout remains top of mind for the industry with AI players continuing to cite power availability as the primary gating factor for AI infrastructure deployment.

While we continue to believe the need for incremental generation and transmission in the U.S. to support the large load community is clear, there has been a recent emphasis on improving the efficiency of existing infrastructure through incremental investments in the grid and generation assets, which suggests a longer growth runway for utility-scale engineering, procurement, and construction, or EPC, providers.

We continue to believe that the magnitude of Quanta's opportunity to capitalize on the increasing tightness within the EPC market is relatively underappreciated, given the company's ability to continually see around corners and understand the next leg of demand from its customers. Target price/base case: $604.

Arm Holdings — ARM-Nasdaq Buy — $134.96 on March 25 by Seaport Research Partners Arm Holdings just announced its long-anticipated AGI CPU, marking its entry into the merchant chip market. In some ways, this marks a major departure from the company's history as a provider of intellectual property.

But in other ways, it is just a continuation of its evolution. Arm guided to fiscal-year 2031 revenue of $25 billion and PF earnings per share of $9 (from $5 billion and $1.78 this year). We see this as risky, but a highly compelling opportunity for the company to greatly enhance its earnings power in a rapidly growing market.

We maintain our Buy rating. Target price: $185.

Braze — BRZE-Nasdaq Outperform — $18.02 on March 25 by Oppenheimer We reiterate our Outperform rating, but reduce our price target to $30 (on lower software multiples) from $40, after Braze's cloud-based software business generated a strong finish to fiscal 2026. Fiscal fourth-quarter 2026 results show impressive bookings growth and continuing organic revenue acceleration driven by broad-based strength. Additionally, the top-line outperformance in fiscal fourth-quarter 2026 flowed through to a record operating margin. This demonstrates the company's ability to grow fast and improve efficiency, while investing aggressively in its AI product suite. Negatively, the initial fiscal-2027 guidance implies business deceleration.

Brinker International — EAT-NYSE Buy — $151.59 on March 24 by Stifel We reiterate our Buy rating, as Brinker International remains poised to sustain comp sales momentum despite temporary fiscal-third-quarter weather disruptions. While winter storms pressured Chili's near-term sales and margins, mobile location data indicates that underlying traffic trends remain robust.

A mid-April chicken sandwich relaunch on the "3 For Me" value platform serves as a near-term catalyst, introducing innovation with a strategic menu redesign. Execution of the operational playbook continues under CEO Kevin Hochman, with a focus on handheld tablet simplification and culinary quality improvements to bolster server retention and guest satisfaction.

Ultimately, a reinforced operational foundation and revitalized culinary program are fueling a powerful marketing flywheel, positioning Chili's for durable growth.

Freshpet — FRPT-Nasdaq Buy — $53.27 on March 24 by Benchmark Equity Research Freshpet continues to enjoy about 94% market share of fresh dog food through the grocery and mass merchant channel, and the model was built to support these channels first (largely through a network of 39,000-plus branded coolers).

[While noting the recent announcement by the Farmer's Dog of a distribution arrangement with Walmart.com, effective as of April,] we foresee challenges for new competitive entrants, especially those with direct-to-consumer models and product offering focused on "customized" food solutions for your dog, to be able to build a material beachhead with a bricks-and-mortar product offering.

Target price: $93.

To be considered for this section, material should be sent to Research@barrons.com.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Space Stocks Jump On Report Of Possible SpaceX IPO Filing
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - SpaceX has positively impacted U.S. space-related stocks following reports indicating that the company may file for an initial public offering within a matter of days, which could potentially value the enterprise at approximately $1.75 trillion.

The anticipated listing could generate over $75 billion, positioning it as one of the largest stock market debuts in history, should it materialize.

Shares of Rocket Lab and Firefly Aerospace experienced an increase exceeding 10 percent, while Intuitive Machines, Planet Labs, Sidus Space, and AST SpaceMobile also recorded significant gains.

Founded in 2002 by Elon Musk, SpaceX has evolved into a prominent aerospace contractor, facilitating rocket launches, satellite services, and engaging in government space contracts.

Additionally, the company oversees Starlink, whose global broadband network has emerged as a significant growth engine for the organization.

data.title: EPA Rescinds Rule Requiring Some Diesel Exhaust Sensors Following Operator Complaints — OPIS
data.description:
EPA has rescinded rules requiring Diesel Exhaust Fluid Urea Quality Sensors on diesel-powered vehicles and nonroad equipment.

EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin said the change was meant to address complaints that sensor failures had caused sudden and sometimes dangerous speed losses and engine shutdowns.

"EPA understands this is a massive issue and has been doing everything in our statutory power to address this. Today, we take another step in furthering our work by removing DEF sensors," he said.

In a TruthSocial post Friday, President Trump touted the new guidelines, saying the "ridiculous" old rules were "a basic disaster."

EPA said the new guidance allows manufacturers and operators to remove the urea quality sensors and switch to nitrous oxide sensors, which the Energy Marketers of America says are less prone to generating inaccurate fault readings.

The guidance also says that NOx sensor-based software updates can be installed on existing engines without being treated as illegal tampering under the Clean Air Act. EPA said this is consistent with its February 2026 Right-to-Repair guidance.

The agency also said the new guidance is expected to "greatly curb errors that traditional sensor technologies have been prone to" and deliver $13.8 billion in total annual savings through reduced repair costs and restored productivity.

EMA said DEF systems have been required on nearly all on-road diesel trucks and much nonroad equipment since 2010 as part of systems to reduce nitrogen oxide emissions.

But fault signals can force vehicles into what are called speed deratements, which can include slowing trucks to as little as 5 mph "causing significant operational disruptions and economic hardship for truckers, farmers, bus operators, and others who depend on diesel-powered equipment," EMA said.

EPA in August extended derate warning windows to allow heavy-duty truck operators travel up to 650 miles or 10 hours before deratement began and allowing normal operation for up to 4,200 miles thereafter.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

  • Reporting by Steven Cronin, scronin@opisnet.com; Editing by Jeffrey Barber, jbarber@opisnet.com

data.title: KPMG plans to cut hundreds of jobs in auditing division, Bloomberg News reports
data.description:
Big Four accounting firm KPMG's UK unit has told nearly 600 staffers in its audit business that their jobs are at risk, Bloomberg News reported on Friday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The company ultimately expects as many as 440 people will leave the business if a redundancy consultation proposal goes ahead, the report said.

KPMG did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.

data.title: PBF Energy Director Sold Shares Worth Over $2.5M
data.description:

data.title: S&P pushes Senegal's local-currency rating deeper into junk on refinancing strains
data.description:
Credit ratings agency S&P on Friday lowered Senegal's local currency rating to "CCC+/C" from "B-/B", citing rising refinancing risks and the government's growing dependence on short-maturity domestic debt amid stalled talks on a new IMF programme.

The agency said Senegal faces gross financing needs of about 26% of GDP in 2026. With IMF funding frozen and access to international markets shut after authorities uncovered previously unreported debts in September 2024, the government has increasingly turned to the regional debt market.

That shift has lifted rollover risks because regional borrowing typically comes with shorter maturities and higher costs.

Senegal has been in economic turmoil since the discovery of hidden liabilities - now estimated at $13 billion - left by the previous administration.

"The 2026 budget, which targets ambitious fiscal consolidation, will prove difficult to execute, as spillovers from the Middle East conflict push up public expenditure, including interest costs, which we estimate at about 25% of government revenue," S&P said in a statement.

Senegal made nearly half-a-billion-dollar debt payments that came due earlier this month, but sources say it has delayed payments to other lenders and faces tough choices on spending cuts that could exacerbate nascent civil unrest.

The agency also revised Senegal's outlook to "negative" from CreditWatch developing.

All three major ratings agencies already classify Senegal's sovereign credit as highly speculative - commonly known as "junk", and S&P's latest downgrade pushes the West African nation further into speculative territory and closer to levels signalling a risk of default.

data.title: Hyperion DeFi Inc reports results for the quarter ended December 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Hyperion DeFi Inc reported a quarterly adjusted loss of $7.76​​ per share for the quarter ended December 31, lower than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of $-6.93. The mean expectation of two analysts for the quarter was for a loss of $4.62 per share.

  • Revenue rose 1,672.3% to $496.23 thousand from a year ago; analysts expected $450.00 thousand.

  • Hyperion DeFi Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was a loss of $7.76​.

  • Hyperion DeFi Inc shares had fallen by 2.0% this quarter.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts was unchanged in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 2 "strong buy" or "buy," no "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the pharmaceuticals peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Hyperion DeFi Inc is $6.13, about 44.2% above its last closing price of $3.42

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data March 27 at 10:13 p.m. UTC. ​All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated. data.description:

data.title: Cybersecurity Stocks Fall After Anthropic AI Security Report
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - The recent report from Anthropic has exerted downward pressure on cybersecurity stocks, stemming from revelations that the company is testing a highly advanced AI model known as Mythos, which possesses sophisticated cyber capabilities and associated security concerns.

The report indicates that the Mythos model represents Anthropic's most powerful system to date, prompting the company to implement a phased release due to apprehensions regarding its potential misuse in cybersecurity scenarios.

The resulting selloff adversely impacted major cybersecurity firms; both CrowdStrike and Palo Alto Networks experienced significant declines, while Zscaler, SentinelOne, Tenable, and Okta also reported considerable losses.

Moreover, the broader cybersecurity sector was affected, with the iShares Cybersecurity and Tech ETF declining by approximately 3 percent.

Investors have become increasingly concerned that the rapid advancements in AI technology may enable more sophisticated cyberattacks and diminish the demand for conventional security tools, particularly as AI systems enhance automated coding and the execution of threats.

data.title: Teachers' Union Advocates For Boycott Of Target For Back-to-School Purchases
data.description:
MINNEAPOLIS (dpa-AFX) - The American Federation of Teachers has urged its 1.8 million members to refrain from shopping at Target for back-to-school items, thereby intensifying pressure on the retailer during a critical sales period.

The union has stated that the boycott is in response to what it perceives as Target's insufficient action regarding federal immigration enforcement measures in Minneapolis, where two U.S. citizens lost their lives during a winter operation.

AFT President Randi Weingarten indicated that the union intends to promote similar resolutions within other prominent organizations, including the AFL-CIO.

Target has asserted its dedication to community investment, emphasizing its long-standing philanthropic efforts and educator discount programs; however, it has not directly addressed the boycott initiative.

This action places additional pressure on Target as CEO Michael Fiddelke seeks to restore growth through store renovations, price reductions, and the expansion of merchandise offerings following three consecutive years of declining annual sales.

data.title: EPA Rescinds Rule Requiring Some Diesel Exhaust Sensors Following Operator Complaints — OPIS
data.description:
EPA has rescinded rules requiring Diesel Exhaust Fluid Urea Quality Sensors on diesel-powered vehicles and nonroad equipment.

EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin said the change was meant to address complaints that sensor failures had caused sudden and sometimes dangerous speed losses and engine shutdowns.

"EPA understands this is a massive issue and has been doing everything in our statutory power to address this. Today, we take another step in furthering our work by removing DEF sensors," he said.

In a TruthSocial post Friday, President Trump touted the new guidelines, saying the "ridiculous" old rules were "a basic disaster."

EPA said the new guidance allows manufacturers and operators to remove the urea quality sensors and switch to nitrous oxide sensors, which the Energy Marketers of America says are less prone to generating inaccurate fault readings.

The guidance also says that NOx sensor-based software updates can be installed on existing engines without being treated as illegal tampering under the Clean Air Act. EPA said this is consistent with its February 2026 Right-to-Repair guidance.

The agency also said the new guidance is expected to "greatly curb errors that traditional sensor technologies have been prone to" and deliver $13.8 billion in total annual savings through reduced repair costs and restored productivity.

EMA said DEF systems have been required on nearly all on-road diesel trucks and much nonroad equipment since 2010 as part of systems to reduce nitrogen oxide emissions.

But fault signals can force vehicles into what are called speed deratements, which can include slowing trucks to as little as 5 mph "causing significant operational disruptions and economic hardship for truckers, farmers, bus operators, and others who depend on diesel-powered equipment," EMA said.

EPA in August extended derate warning windows to allow heavy-duty truck operators travel up to 650 miles or 10 hours before deratement began and allowing normal operation for up to 4,200 miles thereafter.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

  • Reporting by Steven Cronin, scronin@opisnet.com; Editing by Jeffrey Barber, jbarber@opisnet.com
